ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general canned apricot class; the drained-in-syrup variant is not separately enumerated. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Apricots, canned in syrup, drained solids
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category for canned fruit in syrup (drained) used as closest defensible anchor; exact drained-syrup sub-variant may not be separately coded in all FoodEx2 releases.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for canned apricot in syrup (drained); omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 120 g (about 4 apricot halves), drained of syrup before consumption.

nutrient_source.title: Apricots, canned, heavy syrup pack, solids and liquids — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030011 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100g nutrient values are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; the USDA FoodData Central entry for apricots canned in heavy syrup drained (NDB 09021) reports saturated fat of 0.007 g/100g, rounded to 0.01 g/100g and used here as a supplementary approved-source value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 64 for canned apricots in syrup (drained), consistent with values published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) for canned stone fruit in heavy syrup. GL per serving calculated as: GI (64) × carbohydrate per serving (21.31 g/100g × 1.2 serving = 25.57 g) / 100 = 16.4, rounded to 16.3. This is classified as medium GI (56–69).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18.65 g/100g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention despite low sodium (4 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is very low at 4 mg/100g, the high residual sugar from syrup absorption (18.65 g/100g) can promote insulin-mediated sodium retention and fluid retention; individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 60–80 g and prefer apricots canned in water or natural juice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 18.65 g/100g with medium GI of 64 and glycemic load of 16.3 per 120 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high sugar content (18.65 g/100g) and medium GI (64) produce a glycemic load of approximately 16 per standard serving, which is clinically significant for blood glucose management; people with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food and choose fresh or water-packed apricots instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.01 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and low sodium (4 mg/100g), but high sugar warrants moderation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned apricot in syrup (drained) is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sugar content (18.65 g/100g) may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia with regular consumption; limit to small portions and prefer water-packed alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 18.65 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold for pro-inflammatory refined sugar load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The residual syrup sugar absorbed into the fruit flesh (18.65 g/100g) promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation and pro-inflammatory mediator activity relevant to rheumatoid arthritis joint damage; choose fresh apricots or water-packed canned apricots to access carotenoid benefits without the added sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned apricot in syrup (drained) is a commercially preserved stone fruit product in which whole or halved apricots are packed in heavy sugar syrup, heat-sterilised, and then drained of the bulk syrup before consumption or use. The fruit retains a soft, tender texture and a sweet, mildly tart flavour characteristic of ripe apricots, with a golden-orange colour from its high beta-carotene content. Despite draining, the fruit flesh absorbs a significant quantity of syrup sugar during the canning process, resulting in a sugar content of approximately 18.65 g per 100 g — substantially higher than fresh apricots (approximately 9–10 g/100g). The energy content is 83 kcal/100g, with negligible fat (0.11 g/100g) and modest protein (0.64 g/100g). Dietary fibre is 2.7 g/100g, providing some satiety and gut health benefit. Sodium is very low at 4 mg/100g. The food provides useful micronutrients including potassium (143 mg/100g), vitamin A (76 mcg RAE/100g from beta-carotene), vitamin C (3.6 mg/100g), and vitamin E (0.7 mg/100g). The glycemic index is approximately 64 (medium), producing a glycemic load of around 16 per 120 g serving, which is clinically significant. This food is suitable for the general population in small portions but is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high residual sugar content. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Common uses include desserts, fruit salads, yoghurt toppings, and baking. Water-packed or juice-packed alternatives are strongly preferred for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is canned apricot in syrup healthy?
How much sugar is in drained canned apricot?
Can diabetics eat canned apricot in syrup?
Is drained canned apricot good for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of canned apricot?
Difference between canned apricot in syrup and water-packed apricot

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F04030011, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09021 (apricots, canned, heavy syrup, drained solids), an approved secondary source. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication.
notes: 1. Core macros: protein_g (0.64 g) and fat_g (0.11 g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. Carbohydrate_g (21.31 g) is non-zero. No zero-macro justification required. 2. Saturated fat (0.01 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.05 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.02 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100g confirmed negligible) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09021 as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. 3. Vitamin C (3.6 mg), vitamin A (76 mcg RAE), vitamin B1 (0.02 mg), vitamin B2 (0.03 mg), vitamin B3 (0.4 mg), vitamin B6 (0.05 mg), vitamin B12 (0.0 mg — confirmed zero, fruit contains no B12), folate (3 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.0 mcg — confirmed zero, fruit contains no vitamin D), vitamin E (0.7 mg), phosphorus (14 mg), magnesium (8 mg), zinc (0.1 mg), selenium (0.1 mcg), and water (75.5 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 09021 as supplementary approved source. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero; plant-based food contains no cholesterol. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for canned stone fruit in heavy syrup (drained); no Singapore-specific GI measurement identified. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. 7. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; exact drained-syrup sub-variant may not be separately enumerated in all ontology releases.
